([/weɪd ˈdʒaɪlz/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*wayd JYLZE*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key")) is a [romanization system](/wiki/Romanization_of_Chinese "Romanization of Chinese") for [Mandarin Chinese](/wiki/Standard_Chinese "Standard Chinese"). It developed from the system produced by [Thomas Francis Wade](/wiki/Thomas_Francis_Wade "Thomas Francis Wade") during the mid-19th century, and was given completed form with [Herbert Giles](/wiki/Herbert_Giles "Herbert Giles")'s *[A Chinese–English Dictionary](/wiki/A_Chinese%E2%80%93English_Dictionary "A Chinese–English Dictionary")* (1892).

(語言自邇集; 语言自迩集)[[2]](#cite_note-2) in 1867, the first textbook on the [Beijing dialect](/wiki/Beijing_dialect "Beijing dialect") of [Mandarin](/wiki/Mandarin_Chinese "Mandarin Chinese") in English,[[3]](#cite_note-Kaske2008-3) which became the basis for the system later known as Wade–Giles. The system, designed to transcribe Chinese terms for Chinese specialists, was further refined in 1892 by [Herbert Giles](/wiki/Herbert_Giles "Herbert Giles") (in *[A Chinese–English Dictionary](/wiki/A_Chinese%E2%80%93English_Dictionary "A Chinese–English Dictionary")*), a British diplomat in China, and his son [Lionel Giles](/wiki/Lionel_Giles "Lionel Giles"),[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] a curator at the British Museum.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

**Wade–Giles** ([/weɪd ˈdʒaɪlz/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*wayd JYLZE*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key")) is a [romanization system](/wiki/Romanization_of_Chinese "Romanization of Chinese") for [Mandarin Chinese](/wiki/Standard_Chinese "Standard Chinese"). It developed from the system produced by [Thomas Francis Wade](/wiki/Thomas_Francis_Wade "Thomas Francis Wade") during the mid-19th century, and was given completed form with [Herbert Giles](/wiki/Herbert_Giles "Herbert Giles")'s *[A Chinese–English Dictionary](/wiki/A_Chinese%E2%80%93English_Dictionary "A Chinese–English Dictionary")* (1892).

The romanization systems in common use until the late 19th century were based on the [Nanjing dialect](/wiki/Nanjing_dialect "Nanjing dialect"), but Wade–Giles was based on the [Beijing dialect](/wiki/Beijing_dialect "Beijing dialect") and was the system of transcription familiar in the English-speaking world for most of the 20th century. Both of these kinds of transcription were used in [postal romanizations](/wiki/Postal_romanization "Postal romanization") (romanized place-names standardized for postal uses). In [mainland China](/wiki/Mainland_China "Mainland China"), Wade–Giles has been mostly replaced by [Hanyu Pinyin](/wiki/Hanyu_Pinyin "Hanyu Pinyin"), which was officially adopted in 1958, with exceptions for the romanized forms of some of the most commonly used names of locations and persons, and other proper nouns. The romanized name for most locations, persons and other proper nouns in Taiwan is based on the Wade–Giles derived romanized form, for example [Kaohsiung](/wiki/Kaohsiung "Kaohsiung"), the [Matsu Islands](/wiki/Matsu_Islands "Matsu Islands") and [Chiang Ching-kuo](/wiki/Chiang_Ching-kuo "Chiang Ching-kuo").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wade%E2%80%93Giles&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Wade–Giles was developed by [Thomas Francis Wade](/wiki/Thomas_Francis_Wade "Thomas Francis Wade"), a scholar of Chinese and a British ambassador in China who was the first professor of Chinese at the [University of Cambridge](/wiki/University_of_Cambridge "University of Cambridge"). Wade published **Yü-yen Tzŭ-erh Chi** (語言自邇集; 语言自迩集)[[2]](#cite_note-2) in 1867, the first textbook on the [Beijing dialect](/wiki/Beijing_dialect "Beijing dialect") of [Mandarin](/wiki/Mandarin_Chinese "Mandarin Chinese") in English,[[3]](#cite_note-Kaske2008-3) which became the basis for the system later known as Wade–Giles. The system, designed to transcribe Chinese terms for Chinese specialists, was further refined in 1892 by [Herbert Giles](/wiki/Herbert_Giles "Herbert Giles") (in *[A Chinese–English Dictionary](/wiki/A_Chinese%E2%80%93English_Dictionary "A Chinese–English Dictionary")*), a British diplomat in China, and his son [Lionel Giles](/wiki/Lionel_Giles "Lionel Giles"),[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] a curator at the British Museum.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

Taiwan used Wade–Giles for decades as the de facto standard, co-existing with several official romanizations in succession, namely, [Gwoyeu Romatzyh](/wiki/Gwoyeu_Romatzyh "Gwoyeu Romatzyh") (1928), [Mandarin Phonetic Symbols II](/wiki/Mandarin_Phonetic_Symbols_II "Mandarin Phonetic Symbols II") (1986), and [Tongyong Pinyin](/wiki/Tongyong_Pinyin "Tongyong Pinyin") (2000). Under [Ma Ying-jeou](/wiki/Ma_Ying-jeou "Ma Ying-jeou")'s [Kuomintang](/wiki/Kuomintang "Kuomintang") (KMT)-led government, in 2009, Taiwan officially adopted pinyin, and the English romanisations on a number of street signs in cities with then Kuomintang mayors were switched over to conform to this.[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6) However, the majority of the people in Taiwan, both resident and overseas, still use spelling and transcribe their legal names based on the Wade–Giles system, as well as the other aforementioned systems.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

## System features

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wade%E2%80%93Giles&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: System features")]

### Consonants and initial symbols

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wade%E2%80%93Giles&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Consonants and initial symbols")]

A feature of the Wade–Giles system is the representation of the [unaspirated-aspirated](/wiki/Aspiration_(phonetics) "Aspiration (phonetics)") [stop consonant](/wiki/Stop_consonant "Stop consonant") pairs using a character resembling an [apostrophe](/wiki/Apostrophe "Apostrophe"). Thomas Wade and others used the [spiritus asper](/wiki/Spiritus_asper "Spiritus asper") (ʽ or ʻ), borrowed from the [polytonic orthography](/wiki/Polytonic_orthography "Polytonic orthography") of the [Ancient Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek "Ancient Greek") language. [Herbert Giles](/wiki/Herbert_Giles "Herbert Giles") and others used a left (opening) curved single [quotation mark](/wiki/Quotation_mark "Quotation mark") (‘) for the same purpose. A third group used a plain [apostrophe](/wiki/Apostrophe "Apostrophe") ('). The [backtick](/wiki/Backtick "Backtick"), and visually similar characters, are sometimes seen in various electronic documents using the system.

Examples using the spiritus asper: *p*, *pʻ*, *t*, *tʻ*, *k*, *kʻ*, *ch*, *chʻ*. The use of this character preserves *b*, *d*, *g*, and *j* for the romanization of [Chinese varieties](/wiki/Varieties_of_Chinese "Varieties of Chinese") containing [voiced](/wiki/Voiced "Voiced") consonants, such as [Shanghainese](/wiki/Shanghainese "Shanghainese") (which has a full set of voiced consonants) and [Min Nan](/wiki/Min_Nan "Min Nan") (Hō-ló-oē) whose century-old [Pe̍h-ōe-jī](/wiki/Pe%CC%8Dh-%C5%8De-j%C4%AB "Pe̍h-ōe-jī") (POJ, often called Missionary Romanization) is similar to Wade–Giles. POJ, [Legge romanization](/wiki/Legge_romanization "Legge romanization"), [Simplified Wade](/wiki/Simplified_Wade "Simplified Wade"), and [EFEO Chinese transcription](/wiki/EFEO_Chinese_transcription "EFEO Chinese transcription") use the letter ⟨h⟩ instead of an apostrophe-like character to indicate aspiration. (This is similar to the obsolete [IPA](/wiki/International_Phonetic_Alphabet "International Phonetic Alphabet") convention before the [revisions of the 1970s](/wiki/History_of_the_International_Phonetic_Alphabet "History of the International Phonetic Alphabet")). The convention of an apostrophe-like character or ⟨h⟩ to denote aspiration is also found in romanizations of other Asian languages, such as [McCune–Reischauer](/wiki/McCune%E2%80%93Reischauer "McCune–Reischauer") for [Korean](/wiki/Korean_language "Korean language") and [ISO 11940](/wiki/ISO_11940 "ISO 11940") for [Thai](/wiki/Thai_language "Thai language").

People unfamiliar with Wade–Giles often ignore the spiritus asper, sometimes omitting them when copying texts, unaware that they represent vital information. Hanyu Pinyin addresses this issue by employing the Latin letters customarily used for voiced stops, unneeded in Mandarin, to represent the unaspirated stops: *b, p, d, t, g, k, j, q, zh, ch.*

Partly because of the popular omission of apostrophe-like characters, the four sounds represented in Hanyu Pinyin by *j*, *q*, *zh*, and *ch* often all become *ch*, including in many proper names. However, if the apostrophe-like characters are kept, the system reveals a symmetry that leaves no overlap:

* The non-[retroflex](/wiki/Retroflex "Retroflex") *ch* (Pinyin *j*) and *chʻ* (Pinyin *q*) are always before either *ü* or *i*, but never *ih*.
* The [retroflex](/wiki/Retroflex "Retroflex") *ch* (Pinyin *zh*) and *chʻ* (Pinyin *ch*) are always before *ih*, *a*, *ê*, *e*, *o*, or *u*.

### Vowels and final symbols

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wade%E2%80%93Giles&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Vowels and final symbols")]

#### Syllabic consonants

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wade%E2%80%93Giles&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Syllabic consonants")]

Like [Yale](/wiki/Yale_romanization_of_Mandarin "Yale romanization of Mandarin") and [Mandarin Phonetic Symbols II](/wiki/Mandarin_Phonetic_Symbols_II "Mandarin Phonetic Symbols II"), Wade–Giles renders the two types of [syllabic consonant](/wiki/Syllabic_consonant "Syllabic consonant") ([simplified Chinese](/wiki/Simplified_Chinese_characters "Simplified Chinese characters"): 空韵; [traditional Chinese](/wiki/Traditional_Chinese_characters "Traditional Chinese characters"): 空韻; Wade–Giles: *kʻung1-yün4*; [Hanyu Pinyin](/wiki/Pinyin "Pinyin"): *kōngyùn*) differently:

* *-ŭ* is used after the [sibilants](/wiki/Sibilant "Sibilant") written in this position (and this position only) as *tz*, *tzʻ* and *ss* (Pinyin *z*, *c* and *s*).
* *-ih* is used after the [retroflex](/wiki/Retroflex "Retroflex") *ch*, *chʻ*, *sh*, and *j* (Pinyin *zh*, *ch*, *sh*, and *r*).

These finals are both written as *-ih* in [Tongyong Pinyin](/wiki/Tongyong_Pinyin "Tongyong Pinyin"), as *-i* in [Hanyu Pinyin](/wiki/Pinyin "Pinyin") (hence distinguishable only by the initial from [i] as in *li*), and as *-y* in [Gwoyeu Romatzyh](/wiki/Spelling_in_Gwoyeu_Romatzyh "Spelling in Gwoyeu Romatzyh") and [Simplified Wade](/wiki/Simplified_Wade "Simplified Wade"). They are typically omitted in [Bopomofo](/wiki/Bopomofo "Bopomofo").

#### Vowel *o*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wade%E2%80%93Giles&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Vowel o")]

Final *o* in Wade–Giles has two pronunciations in modern Peking dialect: [wo] and [ɤ].

What is pronounced in vernacular Peking dialect as a [close-mid back unrounded vowel](/wiki/Close-mid_back_unrounded_vowel "Close-mid back unrounded vowel") [ɤ] is written usually as *ê*, but sometimes as *o*, depending on historical pronunciation (at the time Wade–Giles was developed). Specifically, after velar initials *k*, *kʻ* and *h* (and a historical *ng*, which had been dropped by the time Wade–Giles was developed), *o* is used; for example, "哥" is *ko1* (Pinyin *gē*) and "刻" is *kʻo4*[[9]](#cite_note-9) (Pinyin *kè*). In Peking dialect, *o* after velars (and what used to be *ng*) have shifted to [ɤ], thus they are written as *ge*, *ke*, *he* and *e* in Pinyin. When [ɤ] forms a syllable on its own, Wade–Giles writes *ê* or *o* depending on the character. In all other circumstances, it writes *ê*.

What is pronounced in Peking dialect as [wo] is usually written as *o* in Wade–Giles, except for *wo*, *shuo* (e.g. "說" *shuo1*) and the three syllables of *kuo*, *kʻuo*, and *huo* (as in 過, 霍, etc.), which contrast with *ko*, *kʻo*, and *ho* that correspond to Pinyin *ge*, *ke*, and *he*. This is because characters like 羅, 多, etc. (Wade–Giles: *lo2*, *to1*; Pinyin: *luó*, *duō*) did not originally carry the medial [w]. Peking dialect does not have phonemic contrast between *o* and *-uo*/*wo* (except in interjections when used alone) and a medial [w] is usually inserted in front of *-o* to form [wo].

### Tones

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wade%E2%80%93Giles&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Tones")]

[Tones](/wiki/Standard_Chinese_phonology#Tones "Standard Chinese phonology") are indicated in Wade–Giles using superscript numbers (1–4) placed after the syllable. This contrasts with the use of diacritics to represent the tones in Pinyin. For example, the Pinyin *qiàn* (fourth tone) has the Wade–Giles equivalent *chʻien4*.

### Punctuation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wade%E2%80%93Giles&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Punctuation")]

Wade–Giles uses [hyphens](/wiki/Hyphen "Hyphen") to separate all syllables within a word (whereas Pinyin separates syllables only in specially defined cases, using hyphens or closing (right) single quotation marks as appropriate).

If a syllable is not the first in a word, its first letter is not [capitalized](/wiki/Majuscule "Majuscule"), even if it is part of a [proper noun](/wiki/Proper_noun "Proper noun"). The use of apostrophe-like characters, hyphens, and capitalization is frequently not observed in place names and personal names. For example, the majority of [overseas](/wiki/Overseas_Taiwanese "Overseas Taiwanese") [Taiwanese people](/wiki/Taiwanese_people "Taiwanese people") write their [given names](/wiki/Given_name "Given name") like "Tai Lun" or "Tai-Lun", whereas the Wade–Giles is actually "Tai-lun". (See also [Chinese names](/wiki/Chinese_name "Chinese name").)

## Comparison with other systems

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wade%E2%80%93Giles&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Comparison with other systems")]

### Pinyin

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wade%E2%80%93Giles&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Pinyin")]

* Wade–Giles chose the [French](/wiki/French_language "French language")-like ⟨j⟩ (implying a sound like IPA's [[ʒ](/wiki/Voiced_postalveolar_fricative "Voiced postalveolar fricative")], as in ***s*** in English *mea**s**ure*) to represent a Northern Mandarin pronunciation of what is represented as ⟨r⟩ in pinyin (Northern Mandarin [[ʐ](/wiki/Voiced_retroflex_fricative "Voiced retroflex fricative")]/ Southern Mandarin [[ɻ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Mandarin "Help:IPA/Mandarin"))
* *Ü* (representing /[y](/wiki/Close_front_rounded_vowel "Close front rounded vowel")/) always has an [umlaut](/wiki/Diaeresis_(diacritic) "Diaeresis (diacritic)") above, while pinyin only employs it in the cases of *nü*, **nüe**, *lü*, **lüe** and **lüan**, while leaving it out after *j*, *q*, *x* and *y* as a simplification because ⟨[u](/wiki/U "U")⟩/[[u](/wiki/Close_back_rounded_vowel "Close back rounded vowel")] cannot otherwise appear after those letters. (The vowel ⟨[u](/wiki/U "U")⟩/[[u](/wiki/Close_back_rounded_vowel "Close back rounded vowel")] can occur in those cases in pinyin where the diaeresis are indicated ⟨[ü](/wiki/%C3%9C "Ü")⟩/[[y](/wiki/Close_front_rounded_vowel "Close front rounded vowel")] or [[ɥ](/wiki/Voiced_labial%E2%80%93palatal_approximant "Voiced labial–palatal approximant")]; in which cases it serves to distinguish the [front vowel](/wiki/Front_vowel "Front vowel") [[y](/wiki/Close_front_rounded_vowel "Close front rounded vowel")] from the [back vowel](/wiki/Back_vowel "Back vowel") [[u](/wiki/Close_back_rounded_vowel "Close back rounded vowel")]. By contrast it is always present to mark the front vowel in Wade–Giles.) Because *yü* (as in 玉 "jade") must have an umlaut in Wade–Giles, the umlaut-less *yu* in Wade–Giles is freed up for what corresponds to *you* ([有](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E6%9C%89 "wikt:有") "have"/"[there is](/wiki/Existential_clause "Existential clause")") in Pinyin.
* The Pinyin cluster ⟨-ong⟩ is ⟨-ung⟩ in Wade–Giles, reflecting the pronunciation of [[ʊ](/wiki/Near-close_near-back_rounded_vowel "Near-close near-back rounded vowel")] as in English *b**oo**k* [/bʊk/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"). (Compare *[kung1-fu](/wiki/Kung_fu "Kung fu")* to *gōngfu* as an example.)
* After a consonant, both Wade–Giles and Pinyin use ⟨-iu⟩ and ⟨-un⟩ instead of the complete syllables: ⟨-iou⟩ and ⟨-uên⟩/⟨-uen⟩.

## Adaptations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wade%E2%80%93Giles&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Adaptations")]

There are several adaptations of Wade–Giles.

### *Mathews*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wade%E2%80%93Giles&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Mathews")]

The romanization system used in the 1943 edition of *[Mathews' Chinese–English Dictionary](/wiki/Mathews%27_Chinese%E2%80%93English_Dictionary "Mathews' Chinese–English Dictionary")* differs from Wade–Giles in the following ways:[[10]](#cite_note-11)

* It uses the right apostrophe: *pʼ*, *tʼ*, *kʼ*, *chʼ*, *tsʼ*, *tzʼŭ*; while Wade–Giles uses the left apostrophe, similar to the aspiration [diacritic](/wiki/Diacritic "Diacritic") used in the [International Phonetic Alphabet before the revisions of the 1970s](/wiki/History_of_the_International_Phonetic_Alphabet "History of the International Phonetic Alphabet"): *pʻ*, *tʻ*, *kʻ*, *chʻ*, *tsʻ*, *tzʻŭ*.
* It consistently uses *i* for the syllable [i], while Wade–Giles uses *i* or *yi* depending on the character.
* It uses *o* for the syllable [ɤ], while Wade–Giles uses *ê* or *o* depending on the character.
* It offers the choice between *ssŭ* and *szŭ*, while Wade–Giles requires *ssŭ*.
* It does not use the spellings *chio*, *chʻio*, *hsio*, *yo*, replacing them with *chüeh*, *chʻüeh*, *hsüeh*, *yüeh* in accordance with their modern pronunciations.
* It uses an underscored *3* to denote a second tone which comes from an original third tone, but only if the following syllable has the neutral tone and the [tone sandhi](/wiki/Tone_sandhi#Mandarin_Chinese "Tone sandhi") is therefore not predictable: *hsiao3•chieh*.
* It denotes the neutral tone by placing a dot (if the neutral tone is compulsory) or a circle (if the neutral tone is optional) before the syllable. The dot or circle replaces the hyphen.

### *Shadick*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wade%E2%80%93Giles&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Shadick")]

In Harold Shadick's *A First Course in Literary Chinese*, the following modifications are made to Wade–Giles:[[11]](#cite_note-12)

* Tones are marked with diacritics instead of tone numbers. The diacritics used are the same as in [Yale](/wiki/Yale_romanization_of_Mandarin "Yale romanization of Mandarin") and [Pinyin](/wiki/Pinyin "Pinyin").
* *ssu* is replaced with *szu*.
* *ê* and *ŭ* are written as *e* and *u*.

The Chinese phrase 文言文入門 (*Wên2-yen2 wên2 ju4-mên2*) would thus be romanized as *Wén-yén wén jù-mén*.

### *Anderson*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wade%E2%80%93Giles&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Anderson")]

Main article: [Simplified Wade](/wiki/Simplified_Wade "Simplified Wade")

This adaptation of Wade–Giles by Olov Anderson has several distinctive features, most notably the use of tonal spelling and the replacement of apostrophes with the letter ⟨h⟩. For example, the aspirated consonants *pʻ*, *tʻ* and *kʻ* become *ph*, *th* and *kh*. Tones are marked by appending the silent letters ⟨v⟩, ⟨x⟩ and ⟨z⟩ to a syllable. Additionally, *ŭ* is written as *y*, and circumflexes and *-h* are dropped in the final position.
